title: Grounding Estimator Guide
summary: Understand circuit amperage, conductor material, target ground resistance, and why grounding assumptions deserve validation.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool provides a planning-level grounding sanity check based on amperage, conductor material, and target resistance.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when documenting early grounding assumptions, reviewing support infrastructure, preparing questions for an electrician or engineer, or checking whether a target seems aggressive enough to need closer review.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the Grounding Estimator and explain how each value affects grounding review pressure.

items.label: Circuit Amperage (A)
items.description: The amperage associated with the circuit or equipment being considered.
items.whyItMatters: Higher amperage can increase the importance of proper bonding, grounding, conductor sizing, and verification.
items.commonMistake: Treating grounding as a generic checkbox regardless of circuit size.

items.label: Conductor Material
items.description: The selected conductor material used for the grounding or bonding assumption.
items.whyItMatters: Material affects conductivity and how the design should be checked against installation requirements.
items.commonMistake: Substituting materials without verifying code, lug ratings, corrosion concerns, and manufacturer requirements.

items.label: Target Ground Resistance (Ω)
items.description: The target resistance value used as the planning goal for the grounding system.
items.whyItMatters: Lower resistance targets can be harder to achieve and may require specialized grounding design or field testing.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a target resistance is met without testing or site-specific validation.

links.label: Relate to power planning
links.note: Use when grounding assumptions are part of a broader electrical load review.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the entered assumptions appear manageable for planning. Watch means the target or amperage deserves review. Risk means the grounding assumption may be too aggressive or too important to treat as a rough estimate.
bullets: Healthy: the assumptions are reasonable as an early planning note.
Watch: verify installation requirements, material choice, and measurement plan.
Risk: involve qualified electrical review and field validation before relying on the result.

sections.title: Grounding validation note
body: Grounding is highly site-specific. Soil conditions, electrode layout, bonding method, corrosion, equipment instructions, and local code requirements can matter more than any simple calculator input.
bullets: Use the result as a prompt for review, not as a final grounding design.
Field measurement and qualified electrical review are the deciding factors.
Document the target and the source of the requirement.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
